provenance.source_corpus: Effects of prescribed fire on habitat of beaver (Castor canadensis) in Elk Island National Park, Canada

source_spans.text: Our results show that repeated burning dramatically decreases beaver lodge occupancy, and that even after one burn the number of active colonies declines and does not recover to pre-fire populations.
Our results show that repeated burning dramatically decreases beaver lodge occupancy, and that even after one burn the number of active colonies declines and does not recover to pre-fire populations.

nodes.name: combination of fire and intensive browsing by ungulates

source_spans.text: In Elk Island National Park, the combination of fire and intensive browsing by ungulates appear to have increased grassland habitats to the detriment of beaver.
In Elk Island National Park, the combination of fire and intensive browsing by ungulates appear to have increased grassland habitats to the detriment of beaver.
Burning in the fall can also dramatically reduce abundance of grasses and woody plants, the winter food supply for many herbivores.

nodes.name: abundance of grasses and woody plants

source_spans.text: Burning in the fall can also dramatically reduce abundance of grasses and woody plants, the winter food supply for many herbivores.

Studies indicate that ungulates are often drawn to areas shortly after a burn ( Vinton et al., 1993; Bailey and Whitham, 2002 ) and the associated increase in foraging by ungulates in burned areas would delay the regeneration of woody plants.

Studies indicate that ungulates are often drawn to areas shortly after a burn ( Vinton et al., 1993; Bailey and Whitham, 2002 ) and the associated increase in foraging by ungulates in burned areas would delay the regeneration of woody plants.
If an area is burned extensively, however, beaver must increase their foraging distances to find unburned vegetation if they wish to remain in the area.
